message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Rock didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the lighthouse keeper, the silence was a companion as constant as the rhythmic *thrum-click* of the great Fresnel lens above him. He had kept the light for thirty years, watching ships pass like ghosts in the mist, never speaking to a soul unless the radio crackled with a distress call.

It was a Tuesday, the kind of gray, heavy day that made the sea look like bruised iron, when he found it.

He was walking the perimeter of the rock, checking for barnacle growth on the foundation, when a glint of green glass caught his eye near the tide line. It wasn’t washed up debris; it was a bottle, sealed with wax that had hardened into a dark, amber scar. The glass was thick, old-fashioned, the kind used for spirits in the early 1900s.

Elias picked it up. It was heavy. He shook it. A single sheet of paper rattled inside.

Back in the keeper’s quarters, the smell of salt and old oil hung in the air. He poured a cup of tea, his hands trembling slightly—not from cold, but from a curiosity he hadn’t felt in decades. He used a knife to carefully pry the cork. It resisted, then gave with a soft *pop*.

He pulled out the paper. It was yellowed, brittle at the edges. The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably familiar.

*“To the Keeper of Blackwood,*

*If you are reading this, I have failed. I am writing this from the cabin of the ‘Aethelgard,’ three days out from port. We are lost. The charts are wrong. The stars are wrong. But I know you. I know you watch. I know you
